people.bio: Richard Allen was a founder member of the Robin Hoods and served as Provincial Grand Secretary of the Freemasons for 21 years. He ran the business Richard Allen and Son, and was a pioneer of photography in Nottingham — the company purchased a Beck Achromatic Stereoscope c.1860 and produced a series of 21 Photographic Views of Nottingham. Their portrait Portret van Helena van Waldeck-Pyrmont met baby (c.1883–84) is held in the Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam. Richard also published Allen's Railway Time Table and Allen's Illustrated First Book, or Reading Made Easy for Children.

occupations.notes: Allen's Railway Time Table; Allen's Illustrated First Book
21 Photographic Views of Nottingham; Beck Achromatic Stereoscope c.1860

people.bio: Mary Ann Deacon married Richard Allen in Basford in 1838. After Richard's death, their daughter Sarah Elizabeth continued to live at Albert Villa.
Sarah Elizabeth Allen continued to live at Albert Villa after the death of her parents Richard and Mary Ann Allen.

Edward Henry Allen worked with his father in Richard Allen and Son. He married Margaret King Furley in Oxford in 1866, and by 1871 the family were living on Hollis Terrace [Holles Crescent?] in The Park. Edward died aged 36 in 1875. There is an Allen Window on the west side of the south transept of St Mary's Church, Nottingham, with a dedication reading: "To the glory of God and in memory of Edward Henry only son of Richard and Mary Ann Allen died Oct 26th 1875 aged 36". Edward Henry Allen is buried in Rock Municipal Cemetery, Nottingham.

Margaret King Furley married Edward Henry Allen in Oxford in 1866. After Edward's death in 1875 she was left to raise their children. She lived at 3 Park Drive in 1881 and 1891, before moving to 'Pendower', 17 Cavendish Crescent North, where she remained in 1901 and 1911.
Mary Adelaide Allen was a daughter of Richard and Mary Ann Allen.

Herbert Edward Allen was the eldest son of Edward Henry and Margaret Allen. In 1881 he and his brother Percy were at Boarding School in Hoveringham. Herbert became a civil engineer. He married Ethel Maude Goddard in Nottingham in 1897 and they moved to Cardiff. They had at least three children: Hildred Herbert Allen, Kenneth Heygate Herbert Allen, and Evelyn Maude Allen. Both sons attended Rossall School, Fleetwood. Kenneth became a Lieutenant-Colonel in the Royal Artillery in 1948.
Ethel Maude Goddard married Herbert Edward Allen in Nottingham in 1897. They moved to Cardiff and had at least three children.
Hildred Herbert Allen was the eldest child of Herbert Edward and Ethel Maude Allen.
Kenneth Heygate Herbert Allen attended Rossall School, Fleetwood. He became a Lieutenant-Colonel in the Royal Artillery in 1948.
Evelyn Maude Allen was the daughter of Herbert Edward and Ethel Maude Allen.

Percy King Allen was the second son of Edward Henry and Margaret Allen. In 1881 he and his brother Herbert were at Boarding School in Hoveringham. Percy trained as an architect. In 1889 he produced a series of architectural drawings of Wollaton Hall, for which he was awarded a RIBA Silver Medal. The drawings were reprinted in The Builder on 13th April 1889.

By 1901 Percy was living in Chester, and by 1911 he was working as an architect in Tunbridge Wells. In 1903 and 1906 he served as Honorary Treasurer of the Nile Mission Press, an organisation dedicated to the printing of evangelical literature in Egypt, which published the quarterly paper Blessed be Egypt.

Percy married Elfreda Jane Hanbury in Richmond, Surrey in 1898. They had a daughter, Meryl Hanbury Allen.

occupations.notes: RIBA Silver Medal 1889 for Wollaton Hall drawings

people.bio: Elfreda Jane Hanbury married Percy King Allen in Richmond, Surrey in 1898. They had one daughter, Meryl Hanbury Allen.
Meryl Hanbury Allen was the daughter of Percy King Allen and Elfreda Jane Hanbury.

Florence Margaret Allen was the youngest child of Edward Henry and Margaret Allen. She continued to live with her mother at 3 Park Drive and later at 'Pendower', 17 Cavendish Crescent North, until at least 1911.

significant_places.description: There is an Allen Window on the west side of the south transept dedicated to the memory of Edward Henry Allen, only son of Richard and Mary Ann Allen, who died 26 October 1875 aged 36.

person_connections.connection: Allen memorial window on west side of south transept

significant_places.name: Rock Cemetery, Nottingham
significant_places.description: Municipal cemetery where Edward Henry Allen is buried.

significant_places.name: Wollaton Hall
significant_places.description: Elizabethan country house now serving as Nottingham Natural History Museum. Percy King Allen produced a series of architectural drawings of Wollaton Hall in 1889, for which he was awarded a RIBA Silver Medal. The drawings were published in The Builder, 13th April 1889.

person_connections.connection: Produced RIBA Silver Medal-winning architectural drawings in 1889, published in The Builder

significant_places.name: Rijksmuseum
significant_places.description: The Rijksmuseum holds the Allen family's portrait Portret van Helena van Waldeck-Pyrmont met baby (c.1883–84), produced using the Beck Achromatic Stereoscope purchased by Richard Allen around 1860.

person_connections.connection: Portrait photograph in the collection: Portret van Helena van Waldeck-Pyrmont met baby, c.1883–84
